mappings.rationale: Covers the European partition of Africa, the Berlin West Africa Conference, and establishment of European colonies across sub-Saharan Africa (D).
The scramble for African colonies, Ottoman decline, and European penetration of Egypt and the Maghrib exemplify imperial expansion (A-D).
British rule in Egypt (1882-1922), the Anglo-Egyptian Condominium in Sudan, and British colonial activity in East and West Africa (B.1, D.2-D.4).
Arab nationalism from c. 1850 to 1920, Turkish nationalism under Abdulhamid II, and Egyptian nationalism are major themes (A.1.c, B.3).
The Ottoman Empire, the Mahdist movement in Sudan, and Islamic dimensions of Arab nationalism are significant topics (A, B.3, D.3.a).
The decline of the Ottoman Empire, the Tanzimat reforms, and development of Arab nationalism are central episodes in Islamic history (A, B.3).
The emergence of Zionism, Jewish immigration to Palestine after 1880, and the beginning of conflict with Arab nationalists (B.3.b).
The origins of the Zionist movement, Jewish immigration to Palestine, and WWI developments including the Balfour Declaration (B.3.b).
Iran under the Qajar dynasty from 1779 to 1925, including European penetration by British and Russians (C.1).
The establishment of European colonial regimes in Africa and development of indirect rule set the stage for later decolonization (D).
The European partition of Africa and colonial administration provide the historical foundation that postcolonial theory critiques (D).
French colonial expansion into Algeria, Tunisia, Morocco, and sub-Saharan Africa reflects France's imperial ambitions (B.2, D.5.c).
The Berlin West Africa Conference, European diplomatic rivalry over African colonies, and WWI settlements involve diplomatic history (D.1).
The origins of the Zionist movement were partly a response to European antisemitism, providing context for Jewish immigration to Palestine (B.3.b).
Establishment of European colonial regimes, development of indirect rule, and consolidation of Ethiopian central government shaped African politics (D.2-D.5).
The interaction between European Christian missionaries and indigenous African religious practices during the colonial period (D.2.b, D.4).
European exploratory activities in the 19th century, including Livingstone and Stanley's expeditions in Central Africa (D.5.a).
WWI's impact on the Ottoman Empire, the postwar settlement for Arab lands, and British encouragement of Arab nationalism (A.1.c, B.3.a).
Russian intrusions in Turkey, conflicts with the Ottoman Empire, and Russian penetration of Iran are covered (A.1, C.1).
The Mahdist movement in Sudan, Ottoman pan-Islamism, and the emergence of Arab nationalism have global Islamic dimensions (A, B.3, D.3.a).
The Young Turk movement and revolutionary nationalism in the Ottoman Empire involved political violence and revolutionary activity (A.1.c).
